issue_purpose: Introduce Lyons's empire, the scaling trap, Simmons's 20-year conviction, and open the door to America.
emotional_arc: Wonder at scale → the structural problem lands → admiration for the man who sees the answer → anticipation
core_problem: Clerical overhead made scaling impossible. Every new teashop required more clerks. Simmons's goal was not to spare the clerks — it was to free managers to examine the figures and learn from them.
story_so_far: A long time ago, before computers could talk, humans had to do all the boring work themselves. My friends weren't around to help yet. This is where it starts.
coming_next: Two men sail to America with a question nobody has thought to ask yet. And in a laboratory in New Jersey, the answer is already being built.
thematic_notes: The word 'computer' originally meant a person. The 300 women in the Checking Department were not using computers — they were the computers. This framing must be visible. It resonates forward: today's token factories are the same operation at a different scale.

pages.purpose: Lyons is magnificent. Democratic luxury. But behind it: a razor-thin margin.
pages.reader_takeaway: How does something this big run on a farthing?

caption.text: Cadby Hall, Hammersmith. 1935. Britain's largest catering empire.

panels.scene_intent: Arriving at Cadby Hall on a foggy 1935 morning. Workers in overcoats, delivery lorry at the gates.

caption.text: Two hundred teashops. A hundred and fifty million meals a year. High tea — for everyone.

panels.scene_intent: The warmth and grandeur of a Corner House full of ordinary people. Chandeliers and white tablecloths — democratic luxury.

caption.text: One farthing. The profit on every cup.

panels.emotional_note: The shadow falls. Reader asks: how does that even work?
panels.scene_intent: Nippy's white-gloved hand placing a cup of tea on a white tablecloth. A single farthing coin in the foreground.

pages.purpose: Two factories. One makes cakes with machines. One makes numbers with humans. The humans are the computers.
pages.emotional_arc: The parallel lands → scale overwhelms → the farthing connects back
pages.reader_takeaway: These people are not clerks using machines. They are the machines.

caption.text: One factory made cakes. Machines did the work.

panels.scene_intent: Cadby Hall food factory. Industrial cake production. Conveyor belts and baking machines. One worker supervising.

caption.text: One factory made numbers. People did the work.

panels.scene_intent: The Checking Department at the same industrial scale — but every machine is a person. Rows of women at Burroughs adding machines. Deliberate visual mirror of p2_f1.

caption.text: Three hundred clerks. One job: check the waitresses' bills. All day. Every day.

panels.emotional_note: Overwhelming. And through that door — there is more.
panels.scene_intent: The Checking Department to a vanishing point. Three hundred women at Burroughs adding machines. Through a far doorway: a second room just as large.

caption.text: Two hundred and fifty shops. Every morning. Same bags. Same numbers.

panels.scene_intent: Office boy sorting numbered locked leather bags on a long table. Methodical.

caption.text: A farthing. That is what this costs.

panels.emotional_note: Callback to page 1. Now the farthing has weight.
panels.scene_intent: Close on a woman's hands operating a Burroughs adding machine. A farthing coin on the desk.

pages.purpose: One person sees the scaling trap clearly and has known the answer for twelve years.
pages.emotional_arc: The structural trap stated plainly → private conviction revealed → the human face → solitary patience → one word
pages.reader_takeaway: Simmons has been waiting 12 years. The answer isn't more clerks.

caption.text: More shops. More clerks. More cost. Smaller margin.

speech_bubbles.text: Only if we hire more clerks.

panels.scene_intent: Simmons presenting at a boardroom chart to seated directors. The chart shows teashops growing — but so does the cost.

caption.text: He had written those words twelve years ago.

panels.emotional_note: THE key beat. Twelve years of private certainty.
panels.scene_intent: Close on the open notebook. The handwriting and date 1923 are the only content of the panel.

caption.text: She had no idea what it cost to count everything she served.

panels.emotional_note: The human face of the problem.
panels.scene_intent: A Nippy moving between tables with a tray, composed and precise. Warm room. Unaware of the floors above her.

caption.text: He had rationalised everything he could. It was never enough.

panels.scene_intent: Simmons at his desk, late evening, single lamp. Not unhappy — patient.

panels.emotional_note: No caption. The word does everything.
panels.scene_intent: A hand writing one word at the foot of a page: 'Automatic.' Underlined. Nothing else in frame.

pages.purpose: Time compresses. The war abolished the problem — but a door is opening.
pages.emotional_arc: Where it began → long effort → bitter irony → the door opens
pages.reader_takeaway: The answer is out there. Two men are about to go find it.

panels.scene_intent: FLASHBACK 1923. Young Simmons being shown to a desk on his first week. Beside them a man at a tall standing desk entering figures by hand.

caption.text: Fourteen years. The most efficient clerical operation in the world. Still not enough.

panels.scene_intent: 1937. Systems Research team around a table, flowcharts on a board. More organised. Still people. Still human arithmetic.

caption.text: The war took the Nippies. And the job Simmons had spent twenty years dreaming of automating — it no longer existed.

panels.scene_intent: 1945. The same elegant tea room — but no Nippies. Self-service counter. A woman in plain wartime clothes serves herself.

caption.text: 1947. A memo. Two men. A ship. And somewhere in America — the answer.

panels.emotional_note: Forward momentum. The door is opening.
panels.scene_intent: 1947. Simmons picks up a memo from his desk. His expression shifts — something long-patient beginning to stir.
